Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917029,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzAyOQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy._core","name":"component: numpy._core","color":"009800","default":false,"description":""},{"id":1710983637,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzEwOTgzNjM3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/triaged","name":"triaged","color":"bc0986","default":false,"description":"Issue/PR that was discussed in a triage meeting"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2019-10-16T19:54:02Z","updated_at":"2024-11-02T22:54:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!-- Please describe the issue in detail here, and fill in the fields below -->

Currently, in order to sort an array in reverse order you have to use some form of [strange hack](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26984414/efficiently-sorting-a-numpy-array-in-descending-order), which may or may not be guaranteed to work in the future.

I suggest adding a reverse parameter to np.sort, similar to the native Python sort() function. This would make the hacks unnecessary and improve readability of code, by making the sorting order explicit.

This feature should not result in any performance problems - after all, the sorting function simply have to reverse the boolean result when comparing values.

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np

a = np.arange(10)
np.random.shuffle(a)
a.sort(reverse = True)
print(a) # should print "[9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0]"
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2453217747","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/14728#issuecomment-2453217747","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728","id":2453217747,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86SOSHT","user":{"login":"tvladyslav","id":16171996,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTcxOTk2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16171996?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav","html_url":"https://github.com/tvladyslav","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-02T22:54:14Z","updated_at":"2024-11-02T22:54:14Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> Regarding reverse-sorting along an axis: does `np.flip(np.sort(x, axis=axis), axis=axis)` do what you want here? Setting aside questions about where `NaN` values end up in reverse-sorted arrays (which is left unspecified in the array API standard), I think this should give the right result.

Yes, it does. I found this discussion and used flip in my code. It works.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2453217747/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917029,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzAyOQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy._core","name":"component: numpy._core","color":"009800","default":false,"description":""},{"id":1710983637,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzEwOTgzNjM3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/triaged","name":"triaged","color":"bc0986","default":false,"description":"Issue/PR that was discussed in a triage meeting"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2019-10-16T19:54:02Z","updated_at":"2024-10-31T14:42:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!-- Please describe the issue in detail here, and fill in the fields below -->

Currently, in order to sort an array in reverse order you have to use some form of [strange hack](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26984414/efficiently-sorting-a-numpy-array-in-descending-order), which may or may not be guaranteed to work in the future.

I suggest adding a reverse parameter to np.sort, similar to the native Python sort() function. This would make the hacks unnecessary and improve readability of code, by making the sorting order explicit.

This feature should not result in any performance problems - after all, the sorting function simply have to reverse the boolean result when comparing values.

### Reproducing code example:

<!-- A short code example that reproduces the problem/missing feature. It should be
self-contained, i.e., possible to run as-is via 'python myproblem.py' -->

```python
import numpy as np

a = np.arange(10)
np.random.shuffle(a)
a.sort(reverse = True)
print(a) # should print "[9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0]"
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2450063089","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/14728#issuecomment-2450063089","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/14728","id":2450063089,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86SCP7x","user":{"login":"tvladyslav","id":16171996,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTcxOTk2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16171996?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav","html_url":"https://github.com/tvladyslav","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tvladyslav/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-31T14:42:37Z","updated_at":"2024-10-31T14:42:37Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Strongly upvote and here is why.
Recently I needed a descending sort function, generic over dimension (3 dimensions, sort across any).
StackOverflow suggests 2 main options:
1. `arr[::-1]`, which is OK, but not applicable to any dimension. I'd need 3 ifs.
2. `-np.sort(-arr)`, which I choose as an elegant working solution.

Now let's try following code:
```python
import numpy as np

def sort_descending():
	shape = (4, 3, 4)
	x = np.arange(np.prod(shape), dtype=np.uint8)
	np.random.shuffle(x)
	x = x.reshape(shape)
	sorted = -np.sort(-x, axis=0)  # descending
	print(sorted)

sort_descending()
```

It works fine with floats and signed integers. But what is the output for `np.uint8`? Here it is:
[[[43 40 37 27]
  [39 22 46  **0**]
  [42 45 31 47]]

 [[41 36 34 20]
  [35 11 38 **44**]
  [18 30 23 24]]

 [[33 25 29 12]
  [ 7  6 15 **13**]
  [17 26 10 14]]

 [[32 21 28  5]
  [ 1  2  3  **4**]
  [16 19  9  8]]]
See something strange? Vector [0, 44, 13, 4] is not sorted. Whole output is correct except one vector!
This happens due to several factors meeting together:

1. Code uses `-` for unsigned type;
2. numpy doesn't warn about negative unsigned value;
3. result is _almost_ correct. It is difficult to catch this problem visually, only test helps;
4. developer has no reverse parameter in `np.sort` and has to use workrounds.

I bet I'm not alone in making this kind of mistake. How many results out there, which are ... _almost_ correct?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2450063089/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
